[{"rating": "4", "title": "Ha! On me! I thought this was a cookbook!", "text": "Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Best beginner book. Been looking for something like this for a long time.", "text": "Looked online for years for something like this. It's the best I've seen.", "review_time": "2020-08-12 11:06:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_8"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Referance Guide", "text": "Good reference guide for the basics", "review_time": "2014-11-13 18:55:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_178"}]

args.code:
# Examine the data to understand book_id and purchase_id patterns
import json
import re

# Load the full results from the files
books_file = var_functions.query_db_5
reviews_file = var_functions.query_db_8

with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Check sample patterns
print(f"Number of books loaded: {len(books_data)}")
print(f"Number of reviews loaded: {len(reviews_data)}")

# Show book_id patterns (first few)
print("\nBook ID patterns:")
for i, book in enumerate(books_data[:5]):
    print(f"  {i+1}. book_id: {book['book_id']}")

# Show purchase_id patterns (first few)
print("\nPurchase ID patterns:")
for i, review in enumerate(reviews_data[:5]):
    print(f"  {i+1}. purchase_id: {review['purchase_id']}")

# Check if the numeric parts match
book_nums = [b['book_id'].split('_')[1] for b in books_data[:10]]
purchase_nums = [r['purchase_id'].split('_')[1] for r in reviews_data[:10]]

print(f"\nBook ID numbers: {book_nums}")
print(f"Purchase ID numbers: {purchase_nums}")

# Extract years from details field
print("\n\nExtracting years from details field:")
years = []
for book in books_data[:20]:
    details = book['details']
    year_matches = []
    
    # Look for year patterns
    year_patterns = [
        r'\b(\d{4})\b',  # 4-digit numbers (likely years)
        r'published.*\b(\d{4})\b',
        r'edition.*\b(\d{4})\b',
    ]
    
    text_lower = details.lower()
    for pattern in year_patterns:
        matches = re.findall(pattern, text_lower)
        if matches:
            year_matches.extend(matches)
    
    # Filter years to be in reasonable range (1800-2025)
    valid_years = [y for y in year_matches if 1800 <= int(y) <= 2025]
    
    # Get decade
    if valid_years:
        primary_year = max(valid_years, key=lambda y: text_lower.index(y))  # earliest match
        decade = f"{int(primary_year)//10*10}s"
        years.append((book['book_id'], book['title'], primary_year, decade))
    else:
        years.append((book['book_id'], book['title'], 'Unknown', 'Unknown'))

for item in years:
    print(f"  {item[0]} - {item[1][:30]}: Year {item[2]}, Decade {item[3]}")

var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
# Examine the data to understand book_id and purchase_id patterns
import json
import re

# Load the full results from the files
books_file = var_functions.query_db_5
reviews_file = var_functions.query_db_8

with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Check sample patterns
print(f"Number of books loaded: {len(books_data)}")
print(f"Number of reviews loaded: {len(reviews_data)}")

# Show book_id patterns (first few)
print("\nBook ID patterns:")
for i, book in enumerate(books_data[:5]):
    print(f"  {i+1}. book_id: {book['book_id']}")

# Show purchase_id patterns (first few)
print("\nPurchase ID patterns:")
for i, review in enumerate(reviews_data[:5]):
    print(f"  {i+1}. purchase_id: {review['purchase_id']}")

# Check if the numeric parts match
book_nums = [b['book_id'].split('_')[1] for b in books_data[:10]]
purchase_nums = [r['purchase_id'].split('_')[1] for r in reviews_data[:10]]

print(f"\nBook ID numbers: {book_nums}")
print(f"Purchase ID numbers: {purchase_nums}")

# Extract years from details field
print("\n\nExtracting years from details field:")
years = []
for book in books_data[:20]:
    details = book['details']
    year_matches = []
    
    # Look for year patterns
    year_patterns = [
        r'\b(\d{4})\b',  # 4-digit numbers (likely years)
        r'published.*\b(\d{4})\b',
        r'edition.*\b(\d{4})\b',
    ]
    
    text_lower = details.lower()
    for pattern in year_patterns:
        matches = re.findall(pattern, text_lower)
        if matches:
            year_matches.extend(matches)
    
    # Filter years to be in reasonable range (1800-2025)
    valid_years = [y for y in year_matches if 1800 <= int(y) <= 2025]
    
    # Get decade
    if valid_years:
        primary_year = max(valid_years, key=lambda y: text_lower.index(y))  # earliest match
        decade = f"{int(primary_year)//10*10}s"
        years.append((book['book_id'], book['title'], primary_year, decade))
    else:
        years.append((book['book_id'], book['title'], 'Unknown', 'Unknown'))

for item in years:
    print(f"  {item[0]} - {item[1][:30]}: Year {item[2]}, Decade {item[3]}")

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
# Examine the data to understand book_id and purchase_id patterns
import json
import re

# Load the full results from the files
books_file = var_functions.query_db_5
reviews_file = var_functions.query_db_8

with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Count records
print('Number of books loaded:', len(books_data))
print('Number of reviews loaded:', len(reviews_data))

# Show book_id patterns
print('\nBook ID patterns:')
for i, book in enumerate(books_data[:5]):
    print(' ', str(i+1) + '. book_id:', book['book_id'])

# Show purchase_id patterns
print('\nPurchase ID patterns:')
for i, review in enumerate(reviews_data[:5]):
    print(' ', str(i+1) + '. purchase_id:', review['purchase_id'])

# Pattern for book IDs both start with bookid_ and purchaseid_
# Numeric part is the key for joining
print('\nSample numeric mappings:')
for i in range(5):
    if i < len(books_data) and i < len(reviews_data):
        book_num = books_data[i]['book_id'].split('_')[1]
        pur_num = reviews_data[i]['purchase_id'].split('_')[1]
        print('  Book', book_num, '<-> Purchase', pur_num)

# Check relationship between these IDs
book_ids = set(b['book_id'].split('_')[1] for b in books_data)
purchase_ids = set(r['purchase_id'].split('_')[1] for r in reviews_data)
print('\nBooks with reviews:', len(book_ids & purchase_ids))
print('Books without reviews:', len(book_ids - purchase_ids))
print('Orphaned reviews:', len(purchase_ids - book_ids))

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re

books_file = var_functions.query_db_5
reviews_file = var_functions.query_db_8

with open(books_file) as f:
    books = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file) as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print('BOOKS:', len(books))
print('REVIEWS:', len(reviews))

print('\nBOOK ID SAMPLES:', [b['book_id'] for b in books[:5]])
print('PURCHASE ID SAMPLES:', [r['purchase_id'] for r in reviews[:5]])

book_nums = set(b['book_id'].split('_')[1] for b in books)
pur_nums = set(r['purchase_id'].split('_')[1] for r in reviews)
print('\nOVERLAP:', len(book_nums & pur_nums))

# Extract publication years
print('\nYEAR EXTRACTION TEST:')
for i in range(10):
    d = books[i]['details']
    title = books[i]['title']
    # Find 4-digit numbers between 1900-2025
    matches = re.findall(r'\b(19[0-9]{2}|20[0-9]{2})\b', d)
    print(title[:25], '-> Years found:', matches)

While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import re

books_file = var_functions.query_db_5
reviews_file = var_functions.query_db_8

with open(books_file) as f:
    books = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file) as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print('BOOKS:', len(books))
print('REVIEWS:', len(reviews))

print('\nBOOK ID SAMPLES:', [b['book_id'] for b in books[:5]])
print('PURCHASE ID SAMPLES:', [r['purchase_id'] for r in reviews[:5]])

book_nums = set(b['book_id'].split('_')[1] for b in books)
pur_nums = set(r['purchase_id'].split('_')[1] for r in reviews)
print('\nOVERLAP:', len(book_nums & pur_nums))

# Extract publication years
print('\nYEAR EXTRACTION TEST:')
for i in range(10):
    d = books[i]['details']
    title = books[i]['title']
    # Find 4-digit numbers between 1900-2025
    matches = re.findall(r'\b(19[0-9]{2}|20[0-9]{2})\b', d)
    print(title[:25], '-> Years found:', matches)

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France

Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. var_functions.query_db:8.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re

# Load data from files
books_file = var_functions.query_db_5
reviews_file = var_functions.query_db_8

with open(books_file) as f:
    books = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file) as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Basic counts
print('Books:', len(books))
print('Reviews:', len(reviews))

# Show a few sample IDs
print('Sample book IDs:', books[0]['book_id'], books[1]['book_id'], books[2]['book_id'])
print('Sample purchase IDs:', reviews[0]['purchase_id'], reviews[1]['purchase_id'], reviews[2]['purchase_id'])

# Extract years from details
sample_years = []
for i in range(min(10, len(books))):
    d = books[i]['details']
    matches = re.findall(r"\b(19[0-9]{2}|20[0-9]{2})\b", d)
    sample_years.append((books[i]['title'][:20], matches))

print('Sample year extraction:')
for title, years in sample_years:
    print(title, '=>', years)

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load data
books_file = var_functions.query_db_24
reviews_file = var_functions.query_db_26

with open(books_file) as f:
    books = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file) as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print('Loaded', len(books), 'books and', len(reviews), 'reviews')

# Extract publication year from details
def extract_year(details):
    # Look for 4-digit years between 1900-2025
    matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    if matches:
        # Return the first match that's likely a publication year
        for match in matches:
            year = int(match)
            if 1900 <= year <= 2025:
                return year
    return None

# Process books to extract years and decades
books_with_years = []
for book in books:
    year = extract_year(book['details'])
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'title': book['title'],
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        })

print('Books with valid years:', len(books_with_years))

# Create mapping from numeric book_id to book info
book_map = {}
for b in books_with_years:
    num_id = b['book_id'].split('_')[1]
    book_map[num_id] = b

print('Books with years in map:', len(book_map))

# Process reviews and join with books
joined_data = []
valid_reviews = 0
for review in reviews:
    pur_id = review['purchase_id'].split('_')[1]
    if pur_id in book_map:
        book = book_map[pur_id]
        joined_data.append({
            'book_id': pur_id,
            'decade': book['decade'],
            'rating': float(review['rating'])
        })
        valid_reviews += 1

print('Reviews matched to books:', valid_reviews)
print('Joined data sample:', joined_data[:5] if joined_data else 'Empty')

# Group by decade
decade_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})
for item in joined_data:
    decade_stats[item['decade']]['ratings'].append(item['rating'])
    decade_stats[item['decade']]['books'].add(item['book_id'])

# Calculate statistics per decade
decade_results = []
for decade, data in decade_stats.items():
    num_books = len(data['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:  # At least 10 distinct books
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        decade_results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_ratings': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating
decade_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('\nDecades with at least 10 books:')
for d in decade_results:
    print(f"{d['decade']}: avg={d['avg_rating']:.3f}, books={d['num_books']}, ratings={d['num_ratings']}")

if decade_results:
    best = decade_results[0]
    print('\nBEST DECADE:', best['decade'])
    print('Average rating:', best['avg_rating'])
else:
    print('No decades meet the criteria')

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load data
books_file = var_functions.query_db_24
reviews_file = var_functions.query_db_26

with open(books_file) as f:
    books = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file) as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print('Loaded', len(books), 'books and', len(reviews), 'reviews')

# Extract publication year from details
def extract_year(details):
    # Look for 4-digit years between 1900-2025
    matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    if matches:
        # Return the first match that's likely a publication year
        for match in matches:
            year = int(match)
            if 1900 <= year <= 2025:
                return year
    return None

# Process books to extract years and decades
books_with_years = []
for book in books:
    year = extract_year(book['details'])
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'title': book['title'],
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        })

print('Books with valid years:', len(books_with_years))

# Create mapping from numeric book_id to book info
book_map = {}
for b in books_with_years:
    num_id = b['book_id'].split('_')[1]
    book_map[num_id] = b

print('Books with years in map:', len(book_map))

# Process reviews and join with books
joined_data = []
valid_reviews = 0
for review in reviews:
    pur_id = review['purchase_id'].split('_')[1]
    if pur_id in book_map:
        book = book_map[pur_id]
        joined_data.append({
            'book_id': pur_id,
            'decade': book['decade'],
            'rating': float(review['rating'])
        })
        valid_reviews += 1

print('Reviews matched to books:', valid_reviews)
print('Joined data sample:', joined_data[:5] if joined_data else 'Empty')

# Group by decade
decade_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})
for item in joined_data:
    decade_stats[item['decade']]['ratings'].append(item['rating'])
    decade_stats[item['decade']]['books'].add(item['book_id'])

# Calculate statistics per decade
decade_results = []
for decade, data in decade_stats.items():
    num_books = len(data['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:  # At least 10 distinct books
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        decade_results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_ratings': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating
decade_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('\nDecades with at least 10 books:')
for d in decade_results:
    print(f"{d['decade']}: avg={d['avg_rating']:.3f}, books={d['num_books']}, ratings={d['num_ratings']}")

if decade_results:
    best = decade_results[0]
    print('\nBEST DECADE:', best['decade'])
    print('Average rating:', best['avg_rating'])
else:
    print('No decades meet the criteria')

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load data from files
books_file = var_functions.query_db_24
reviews_file = var_functions.query_db_26

with open(books_file) as f:
    books = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file) as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print('Loaded books:', len(books))
print('Loaded reviews:', len(reviews))

# Extract publication year from details text
def extract_year(details_text):
    year_matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details_text)
    if year_matches:
        for match in year_matches:
            year_val = int(match)
            if 1900 <= year_val <= 2025:
                return year_val
    return None

# Process books to get publication years
books_with_years = []
for book_item in books:
    year_val = extract_year(book_item['details'])
    if year_val:
        decade_str = str(year_val // 10 * 10) + 's'
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book_item['book_id'],
            'year': year_val,
            'decade': decade_str
        })

print('Books with extractable years:', len(books_with_years))

# Map numeric book IDs to book data
book_id_map = {}
for book_item in books_with_years:
    numeric_id = book_item['book_id'].split('_')[1]
    book_id_map[numeric_id] = book_item

print('Books in ID map:', len(book_id_map))

# Join reviews with books by matching numeric IDs
joined_records = []
review_count = 0
for review_item in reviews:
    review_numeric_id = review_item['purchase_id'].split('_')[1]
    if review_numeric_id in book_id_map:
        book_data = book_id_map[review_numeric_id]
        joined_records.append({
            'book_id': review_numeric_id,
            'decade': book_data['decade'],
            'rating': float(review_item['rating'])
        })
        review_count += 1

print('Reviews successfully joined:', review_count)
print('Sample joined record:', joined_records[0] if joined_records else 'None')

# Group by decade and calculate statistics
decade_group = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'unique_books': set()})
for record in joined_records:
    decade_group[record['decade']]['ratings'].append(record['rating'])
    decade_group[record['decade']]['unique_books'].add(record['book_id'])

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books and calculate averages
valid_decades = []
for decade, data in decade_group.items():
    book_count = len(data['unique_books'])
    if book_count >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        valid_decades.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'average_rating': avg_rating,
            'book_count': book_count,
            'rating_count': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
valid_decades.sort(key=lambda x: x['average_rating'], reverse=True)

print('\nAll valid decades:')
for d in valid_decades:
    print(d['decade'], 'avg=' + str(round(d['average_rating'], 3)),
          'books=' + str(d['book_count']), 
          'ratings=' + str(d['rating_count']))

if valid_decades:
    best_decade = valid_decades[0]
    print('\nTop decade:', best_decade['decade'])
    print('Average rating:', best_decade['average_rating'])
else:
    print('No decades found with at least 10 books')

            'book_id': book_item['book_id'],
            'year': year_val,
            'decade': decade_str
        })

print('Books with extractable years:', len(books_with_years))

# Map numeric book IDs to book data
book_id_map = {}
for book_item in books_with_years:
    numeric_id = book_item['book_id'].split('_')[1]
    book_id_map[numeric_id] = book_item

print('Books in ID map:', len(book_id_map))

# Join reviews with books by matching numeric IDs
joined_records = []
review_count = 0
for review_item in reviews:
    review_numeric_id = review_item['purchase_id'].split('_')[1]
    if review_numeric_id in book_id_map:
        book_data = book_id_map[review_numeric_id]
        joined_records.append({
            'book_id': review_numeric_id,
            'decade': book_data['decade'],
            'rating': float(review_item['rating'])
        })
        review_count += 1

print('Reviews successfully joined:', review_count)
print('Sample joined record:', joined_records[0] if joined_records else 'None')

# Group by decade and calculate statistics
decade_group = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'unique_books': set()})
for record in joined_records:
    decade_group[record['decade']]['ratings'].append(record['rating'])
    decade_group[record['decade']]['unique_books'].add(record['book_id'])

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books and calculate averages
valid_decades = []
for decade, data in decade_group.items():
    book_count = len(data['unique_books'])
    if book_count >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        valid_decades.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'average_rating': avg_rating,
            'book_count': book_count,
            'rating_count': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
valid_decades.sort(key=lambda x: x['average_rating'], reverse=True)

print('\nAll valid decades:')
for d in valid_decades:
    print(d['decade'], 'avg=' + str(round(d['average_rating'], 3)),
          'books=' + str(d['book_count']), 
          'ratings=' + str(d['rating_count']))

if valid_decades:
    best_decade = valid_decades[0]
    print('\nTop decade:', best_decade['decade'])
    print('Average rating:', best_decade['average_rating'])
else:
    print('No decades found with at least 10 books')

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict
import os

# Load data from the files
books_path = '/tmp/tmpf8c7v7x8.json'  # This is the file path from query_db:24
reviews_path = '/tmp/tmphs31p8h4.json'  # This is the file path from query_db:26

if not os.path.exists(books_path):
    books_path = var_functions.query_db_24
if not os.path.exists(reviews_path):
    reviews_path = var_functions.query_db_26

with open(books_path) as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(reviews_path) as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print('Books loaded:', len(books))
print('Reviews loaded:', len(reviews))

# Function to extract decade from book details
def get_decade(details_text):
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details_text)
    for year_str in years:
        year = int(year_str)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2025:
            decade = (year // 10) * 10
            return str(decade) + 's'
    return None

# Extract decades from books
books_info = []
for book in books:
    decade = get_decade(book['details'])
    if decade:
        book_num_id = book['book_id'].split('_')[1]
        books_info.append({'id': book_num_id, 'decade': decade})

print('Books with valid decades:', len(books_info))

# Create mapping from numeric ID to decade
book_decade_map = {}
for info in books_info:
    book_decade_map[info['id']] = info['decade']

print('Books in decade map:', len(book_decade_map))

# Join reviews with book decades
joined_data = []
for review in reviews:
    review_num_id = review['purchase_id'].split('_')[1]
    if review_num_id in book_decade_map:
        joined_data.append({
            'book_id': review_num_id,
            'decade': book_decade_map[review_num_id],
            'rating': float(review['rating'])
        })

print('Reviews matched to books:', len(joined_data))

# Group by decade
decades = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})
for item in joined_data:
    decades[item['decade']]['ratings'].append(item['rating'])
    decades[item['decade']]['books'].add(item['book_id'])

print('Different decades found:', len(decades))

# Calculate averages and filter by minimum 10 books
results = []
for decade, data in decades.items():
    num_books = len(data['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_ratings': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('Decades with >=10 books:', len(results))
print('\nRanking:')
for idx, r in enumerate(results, 1):
    print(f"{idx}. {r['decade']}: avg={r['avg_rating']:.3f}, books={r['num_books']}, ratings={r['num_ratings']}")

if results:
    best = results[0]
    best_decade = best['decade']
    best_avg = best['avg_rating']
    print(f'\nBEST DECADE: {best_decade} with average rating {best_avg:.3f}')
else:
    print('No decades meet the criteria')
    best_decade = None

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(best_decade))

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json, re
from collections import defaultdict

books_path = var_functions.query_db_24
reviews_path = var_functions.query_db_26

with open(books_path) as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(reviews_path) as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print('Loaded', len(books), 'books and', len(reviews), 'reviews')

# Extract decades
def extract_decade(detail):
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', detail)
    for y in years:
        yr = int(y)
        if 1900 <= yr <= 2025:
            return str(yr//10*10) + 's'
    return None

books_with_decades = []
for b in books:
    dec = extract_decade(b['details'])
    if dec:
        books_with_decades.append({'id': b['book_id'].split('_')[1], 'decade': dec})

print('Books with valid decades:', len(books_with_decades))

# Map to decade
book2decade = {}
for bd in books_with_decades:
    book2decade[bd['id']] = bd['decade']

# Join with reviews
joined = []
for r in reviews:
    rid = r['purchase_id'].split('_')[1]
    if rid in book2decade:
        joined.append({'book': rid, 'decade': book2decade[rid], 'rating': float(r['rating'])})

print('Matched reviews to books:', len(joined))

# Group and calculate
groups = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})
for j in joined:
    groups[j['decade']]['ratings'].append(j['rating'])
    groups[j['decade']]['books'].add(j['book'])

print('Different decades found:', len(groups))

# Filter and rank
valid = []
for d, data in groups.items():
    if len(data['books']) >= 10:
        avg = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        valid.append({'decade': d, 'avg': avg, 'books': len(data['books']), 'ratings': len(data['ratings'])})

valid.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg'], reverse=True)

print('Decades with at least 10 books:', len(valid))
for idx, v in enumerate(valid, 1):
    print(str(idx) + '.', v['decade'], 'avg=' + str(round(v['avg'], 3)))

answer = valid[0]['decade'] if valid else None
print('FINAL ANSWER:', answer)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({'decade': answer, 'count': len(valid)}))

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Referance Guide
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Good reference guide for the basics

val_args.code:
import json, re
from collections import defaultdict

books_path = var_functions.query_db_24
reviews_path = var_functions.query_db_26

with open(books_path) as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(reviews_path) as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print('Loaded', len(books), 'books and', len(reviews), 'reviews')

# Extract decades
def extract_decade(detail):
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', detail)
    for y in years:
        yr = int(y)
        if 1900 <= yr <= 2025:
            return str(yr//10*10) + 's'
    return None

books_with_decades = []
for b in books:
    dec = extract_decade(b['details'])
    if dec:
        books_with_decades.append({'id': b['book_id'].split('_')[1], 'decade': dec})

print('Books with valid decades:', len(books_with_decades))

# Map to decade
book2decade = {}
for bd in books_with_decades:
    book2decade[bd['id']] = bd['decade']

# Join with reviews
joined = []
for r in reviews:
    rid = r['purchase_id'].split('_')[1]
    if rid in book2decade:
        joined.append({'book': rid, 'decade': book2decade[rid], 'rating': float(r['rating'])})

print('Matched reviews to books:', len(joined))

# Group and calculate
groups = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})
for j in joined:
    groups[j['decade']]['ratings'].append(j['rating'])
    groups[j['decade']]['books'].add(j['book'])

print('Different decades found:', len(groups))

# Filter and rank
valid = []
for d, data in groups.items():
    if len(data['books']) >= 10:
        avg = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        valid.append({'decade': d, 'avg': avg, 'books': len(data['books']), 'ratings': len(data['ratings'])})

valid.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg'], reverse=True)

print('Decades with at least 10 books:', len(valid))
for idx, v in enumerate(valid, 1):
    print(str(idx) + '.', v['decade'], 'avg=' + str(round(v['avg'], 3)))

answer = valid[0]['decade'] if valid else None
print('FINAL ANSWER:', answer)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({'decade': answer, 'count': len(valid)}))

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load data using the direct variable names from storage
with open(query_db_24) as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(query_db_26) as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print('Loaded', len(books), 'books and', len(reviews), 'reviews')

# Extract publication decade from book details
def get_decade(detail_text):
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', detail_text)
    for y in years:
        year_num = int(y)
        if 1900 <= year_num <= 2025:
            decade_num = year_num // 10 * 10
            return str(decade_num) + 's'
    return None

# Build book decade lookup
book_decades = {}
for book in books:
    decade = get_decade(book['details'])
    if decade:
        book_id_num = book['book_id'].split('_')[1]
        book_decades[book_id_num] = decade

print('Books with valid decades:', len(book_decades))

# Join with reviews
joined = []
for review in reviews:
    review_id_num = review['purchase_id'].split('_')[1]
    if review_id_num in book_decades:
        joined.append({
            'book_id': review_id_num,
            'decade': book_decades[review_id_num],
            'rating': float(review['rating'])
        })

print('Reviews matched to books:', len(joined))

# Group by decade
groups = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})
for item in joined:
    groups[item['decade']]['ratings'].append(item['rating'])
    groups[item['decade']]['books'].add(item['book_id'])

print('Different decades:', len(groups))

# Calculate averages and filter by minimum 10 books
results = []
for decade, data in groups.items():
    num_books = len(data['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'book_count': num_books,
            'rating_count': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('\nDecades with at least 10 books:')
for idx, r in enumerate(results, 1):
    print(f"{idx}. {r['decade']}: avg={r['avg_rating']:.3f}, books={r['book_count']}, ratings={r['rating_count']}")

best_decade = results[0]['decade'] if results else None
print('\nBEST DECADE:', best_decade)

result_data = {'decade': best_decade, 'ranking': results}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result_data))

While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer.
