val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load data using the direct variable names from storage
with open(query_db_24) as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(query_db_26) as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print('Loaded', len(books), 'books and', len(reviews), 'reviews')

# Extract publication decade from book details
def get_decade(detail_text):
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', detail_text)
    for y in years:
        year_num = int(y)
        if 1900 <= year_num <= 2025:
            decade_num = year_num // 10 * 10
            return str(decade_num) + 's'
    return None

# Build book decade lookup
book_decades = {}
for book in books:
    decade = get_decade(book['details'])
    if decade:
        book_id_num = book['book_id'].split('_')[1]
        book_decades[book_id_num] = decade

print('Books with valid decades:', len(book_decades))

# Join with reviews
joined = []
for review in reviews:
    review_id_num = review['purchase_id'].split('_')[1]
    if review_id_num in book_decades:
        joined.append({
            'book_id': review_id_num,
            'decade': book_decades[review_id_num],
            'rating': float(review['rating'])
        })

print('Reviews matched to books:', len(joined))

# Group by decade
groups = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})
for item in joined:
    groups[item['decade']]['ratings'].append(item['rating'])
    groups[item['decade']]['books'].add(item['book_id'])

print('Different decades:', len(groups))

# Calculate averages and filter by minimum 10 books
results = []
for decade, data in groups.items():
    num_books = len(data['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'book_count': num_books,
            'rating_count': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('\nDecades with at least 10 books:')
for idx, r in enumerate(results, 1):
    print(f"{idx}. {r['decade']}: avg={r['avg_rating']:.3f}, books={r['book_count']}, ratings={r['rating_count']}")

best_decade = results[0]['decade'] if results else None
print('\nBEST DECADE:', best_decade)

result_data = {'decade': best_decade, 'ranking': results}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result_data))

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Referance Guide
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Good reference guide for the basics

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Access the stored results using the correct keys
books_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:40']
reviews_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:26']

# Parse the JSON data
books = json.loads(books_data) if isinstance(books_data, str) else books_data
reviews = json.loads(reviews_data) if isinstance(reviews_data, str) else reviews_data

print('Loaded', len(books), 'books and', len(reviews), 'reviews')

# Extract publication decade from book details
def get_decade(detail_text):
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', detail_text)
    for y in years:
        year_num = int(y)
        if 1900 <= year_num <= 2025:
            decade_num = year_num // 10 * 10
            return str(decade_num) + 's'
    return None

# Build mapping from numeric book ID to decade
book_decades = {}
for book in books:
    decade = get_decade(book['details'])
    if decade:
        book_id_num = book['book_id'].split('_')[1]
        book_decades[book_id_num] = decade

print('Books with valid decades:', len(book_decades))

# Join reviews with books
joined = []
for review in reviews:
    review_id_num = review['purchase_id'].split('_')[1]
    if review_id_num in book_decades:
        joined.append({
            'book_id': review_id_num,
            'decade': book_decades[review_id_num],
            'rating': float(review['rating'])
        })

print('Reviews matched to books:', len(joined))

# Group by decade
groups = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})
for item in joined:
    groups[item['decade']]['ratings'].append(item['rating'])
    groups[item['decade']]['books'].add(item['book_id'])

print('Different decades:', len(groups))

# Calculate averages and filter by minimum 10 books
results = []
for decade, data in groups.items():
    num_books = len(data['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'book_count': num_books,
            'rating_count': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('\nDecades with at least 10 books:')
for idx, r in enumerate(results, 1):
    print(str(idx) + '. ' + r['decade'] + ': avg=' + str(round(r['avg_rating'], 3)) + ', books=' + str(r['book_count']) + ', ratings=' + str(r['rating_count']))

best_decade = results[0]['decade'] if results else None
print('\nBEST DECADE:', best_decade)

result_data = {'decade': best_decade}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result_data))

args.code:
import json, re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load the data from the stored results
books_path = '/tmp/tmpf8c7v7x8.json'
reviews_path = '/tmp/tmphs31p8h4.json'

with open(books_path) as f:
    books = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_path) as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print('Books:', len(books), 'Reviews:', len(reviews))

# Extract decade from book details
book_decades = {}
for b in books:
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', b['details'])
    for y in years:
        year = int(y)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2025:
            decade = str(year // 10 * 10) + 's'
            book_decades[b['book_id'].split('_')[1]] = decade
            break

print('Books with decades:', len(book_decades))

# Join with reviews
joined = []
for r in reviews:
    rid = r['purchase_id'].split('_')[1]
    if rid in book_decades:
        joined.append((rid, book_decades[rid], float(r['rating'])))

print('Joined records:', len(joined))

# Group by decade
groups = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})
for book_id, decade, rating in joined:
    groups[decade]['ratings'].append(rating)
    groups[decade]['books'].add(book_id)

print('Decades found:', len(groups))

# Calculate averages, filter by >=10 books
results = []
for decade, data in groups.items():
    if len(data['books']) >= 10:
        avg = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        results.append((decade, avg, len(data['books']), len(data['ratings'])))

results.sort(key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)

print('Valid decades:', len(results))
for r in results:
    print(r[0], 'avg=' + str(round(r[1], 3)), 'books=' + str(r[2]), 'ratings=' + str(r[3]))

best_decade = results[0][0] if results else None
print('BEST:', best_decade)

output = {'decade': best_decade}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))

Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json, re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load the data from the stored results
books_path = '/tmp/tmpf8c7v7x8.json'
reviews_path = '/tmp/tmphs31p8h4.json'

with open(books_path) as f:
    books = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_path) as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print('Books:', len(books), 'Reviews:', len(reviews))

# Extract decade from book details
book_decades = {}
for b in books:
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', b['details'])
    for y in years:
        year = int(y)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2025:
            decade = str(year // 10 * 10) + 's'
            book_decades[b['book_id'].split('_')[1]] = decade
            break

print('Books with decades:', len(book_decades))

# Join with reviews
joined = []
for r in reviews:
    rid = r['purchase_id'].split('_')[1]
    if rid in book_decades:
        joined.append((rid, book_decades[rid], float(r['rating'])))

print('Joined records:', len(joined))

# Group by decade
groups = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})
for book_id, decade, rating in joined:
    groups[decade]['ratings'].append(rating)
    groups[decade]['books'].add(book_id)

print('Decades found:', len(groups))

# Calculate averages, filter by >=10 books
results = []
for decade, data in groups.items():
    if len(data['books']) >= 10:
        avg = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        results.append((decade, avg, len(data['books']), len(data['ratings'])))

results.sort(key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)

print('Valid decades:', len(results))
for r in results:
    print(r[0], 'avg=' + str(round(r[1], 3)), 'books=' + str(r[2]), 'ratings=' + str(r[3]))

best_decade = results[0][0] if results else None
print('BEST:', best_decade)

output = {'decade': best_decade}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))

import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Access the stored data using the correct key names
books_data = globals()['var_functions.query_db:40']
reviews_data = globals()['var_functions.query_db:26']

# Load JSON data
books = json.loads(books_data) if isinstance(books_data, str) else books_data
reviews = json.loads(reviews_data) if isinstance(reviews_data, str) else reviews_data

print('Loaded {} books and {} reviews'.format(len(books), len(reviews)))

# Extract publication decade from book details
def extract_decade(detail_text):
    matches = re.findall(r'\\b(19\\d{2}|20[0-2]\\d)\\b', detail_text)
    for match in matches:
        year = int(match)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2025:
            decade = (year // 10) * 10
            return str(decade) + 's'
    return None

# Build mapping from book numeric ID to decade
book_decades = {}
book_count = 0
for book in books:
    decade = extract_decade(book['details'])
    if decade:
        book_id_num = book['book_id'].split('_')[1]
        book_decades[book_id_num] = decade
        book_count += 1

print('Books with valid decades: {}'.format(book_count))

# Join reviews with books using numeric IDs
joined = []
review_count = 0
for review in reviews:
    review_id_num = review['purchase_id'].split('_')[1]
    if review_id_num in book_decades:
        joined.append({
            'book_id': review_id_num,
            'decade': book_decades[review_id_num],
            'rating': float(review['rating'])
        })
        review_count += 1

print('Matched reviews to books: {}'.format(review_count))

# Group by decade
groups = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})
for item in joined:
    decade = item['decade']
    groups[decade]['ratings'].append(item['rating'])
    groups[decade]['books'].add(item['book_id'])

print('Different decades found: {}'.format(len(groups)))

# Calculate averages and filter by minimum 10 distinct books
results = []
for decade, data in groups.items():
    num_books = len(data['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'book_count': num_books,
            'rating_count': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating
def get_avg(r):
    return r['avg_rating']

results.sort(key=get_avg, reverse=True)

print('\\nDecades with at least 10 books:')
for idx, r in enumerate(results):
    print('{} - {}: avg={}, books={}, ratings={}'.format(
        idx + 1, r['decade'], round(r['avg_rating'], 3), 
        r['book_count'], r['rating_count']
    ))

best = results[0] if results else None
best_decade = best['decade'] if best else None

print('\\nBEST DECADE: {}'.format(best_decade))

output = {'decade': best_decade}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))

        idx + 1, r['decade'], round(r['avg_rating'], 3),

    ))

best = results[0] if results else None
best_decade = best['decade'] if best else None

print('\\nBEST DECADE: {}'.format(best_decade))

output = {'decade': best_decade}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load all books and reviews from the stored query results
books_file = var_functions.query_db_40
reviews_file = var_functions.query_db_26

# Read the JSON files
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print(f"Loaded {len(books)} books and {len(reviews)} reviews")

# Extract publication year from book details
def extract_year(details_text):
    # Find 4-digit years between 1900-2025
    matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details_text)
    if matches:
        for match in matches:
            year = int(match)
            if 1900 <= year <= 2025:
                return year
    return None

# Build book to decade mapping
book_to_decade = {}
books_with_years = 0
for book in books:
    year = extract_year(book['details'])
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        # Extract numeric ID from bookid_X
        book_num_id = book['book_id'].split('_')[1]
        book_to_decade[book_num_id] = decade
        books_with_years += 1

print(f"Books with extractable years: {books_with_years}")

# Join reviews with books using numeric IDs
joined_reviews = []
for review in reviews:
    review_num_id = review['purchase_id'].split('_')[1]
    if review_num_id in book_to_decade:
        joined_reviews.append({
            'book_id': review_num_id,
            'decade': book_to_decade[review_num_id],
            'rating': float(review['rating'])
        })

print(f"Successfully joined {len(joined_reviews)} reviews to books")

# Group by decade and calculate statistics
decade_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})
for review in joined_reviews:
    decade_stats[review['decade']]['ratings'].append(review['rating'])
    decade_stats[review['decade']]['books'].add(review['book_id'])

print(f"Found {len(decade_stats)} different decades")

# Calculate average ratings and filter by at least 10 distinct books
decade_results = []
for decade, data in decade_stats.items():
    num_books = len(data['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        decade_results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_ratings': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print(f"\nDecades with at least 10 books ({len(decade_results)} found):")
for i, result in enumerate(decade_results, 1):
    print(f"{i}. {result['decade']}: avg_rating={result['avg_rating']:.3f}, "
          f"books={result['num_books']}, ratings={result['num_ratings']}")

# Get the best decade
if decade_results:
    best_decade = decade_results[0]['decade']
    best_avg = decade_results[0]['avg_rating']
    print(f"\nBEST DECADE: {best_decade} with average rating {best_avg:.3f}")
else:
    best_decade = None
    print("\nNo decades found with at least 10 books")

# Return the result
result = {'best_decade': best_decade}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_ratings': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating
def sort_key(x):
    return x['avg_rating']

results.sort(key=sort_key, reverse=True)

print('Decades with at least 10 books: {}'.format(len(results)))

    ))

best_decade = results[0]['decade'] if results else None
print('\nBest decade: {}'.format(best_decade))

result_dict = {'best_decade': best_decade}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result_dict))

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load data from files
books_file = var_functions.query_db_40
reviews_file = var_functions.query_db_26

with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print('Loaded {} books and {} reviews'.format(len(books), len(reviews)))

# Extract decade from book details
book_decades = {}
for book in books:
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', book['details'])
    for year_str in years:
        year = int(year_str)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2025:
            decade = str(year // 10 * 10) + 's'
            book_num_id = book['book_id'].split('_')[1]
            book_decades[book_num_id] = decade
            break

print('Books with valid decades: {}'.format(len(book_decades)))

# Join reviews with books
joined = []
for review in reviews:
    review_num_id = review['purchase_id'].split('_')[1]
    if review_num_id in book_decades:
        joined.append({
            'book_id': review_num_id,
            'decade': book_decades[review_num_id],
            'rating': float(review['rating'])
        })

print('Successfully joined {} reviews'.format(len(joined)))

# Group by decade
groups = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})
for item in joined:
    groups[item['decade']]['ratings'].append(item['rating'])
    groups[item['decade']]['books'].add(item['book_id'])

print('Found {} different decades'.format(len(groups)))

# Calculate decade statistics
results = []
for decade, data in groups.items():
    num_books = len(data['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_ratings': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating
def sort_key(x):
    return x['avg_rating']

results.sort(key=sort_key, reverse=True)

print('Decades with at least 10 books: {}'.format(len(results)))
for i, r in enumerate(results):
    avg_str = str(round(r['avg_rating'], 3))
    print('{}. {}: avg={}, books={}, ratings={}'.format(
        i + 1, r['decade'], avg_str, r['num_books'], r['num_ratings']
    ))

best_decade = results[0]['decade'] if results else None
print('\nBest decade: {}'.format(best_decade))

result_dict = {'best_decade': best_decade}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result_dict))

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Referance Guide
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Good reference guide for the basics

val_args.code:
import json, re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load all books and reviews
books_file = var_functions.query_db_40
reviews_file = var_functions.query_db_26

with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print('Loaded {} books and {} reviews'.format(len(books), len(reviews)))

# Extract decades from books
book_decades = {}
for book in books:
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', book['details'])
    for y in years:
        year = int(y)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2025:
            book_decades[book['book_id'].split('_')[1]] = str(year // 10 * 10) + 's'
            break

print('Books with decades: {}'.format(len(book_decades)))

# Join with reviews
joined = []
for r in reviews:
    rid = r['purchase_id'].split('_')[1]
    if rid in book_decades:
        joined.append((rid, book_decades[rid], float(r['rating'])))

print('Joined: {}'.format(len(joined)))

# Group and calculate
stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})
for book_id, decade, rating in joined:
    stats[decade]['ratings'].append(rating)
    stats[decade]['books'].add(book_id)

print('Decades found: {}'.format(len(stats)))

# Filter and rank
valid = []
for decade, data in stats.items():
    num_books = len(data['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        valid.append((decade, avg, num_books, len(data['ratings'])))

valid.sort(key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)

print('Valid decades: {}'.format(len(valid)))
for i, (decade, avg, books, ratings) in enumerate(valid):
    print('{}. {} avg={} books={} ratings={}'.format(i+1, decade, round(avg, 3), books, ratings))

best = valid[0][0] if valid else None
print('Best decade: {}'.format(best))

result = {'decade': best}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load data from the stored files
books_file = var_functions.query_db_62
reviews_file = var_functions.query_db_64

with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print('Loaded {} books and {} reviews'.format(len(books), len(reviews)))

# Function to extract publication year from book details
def extract_year(details_text):
    # Find all 4-digit years in the text
    matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details_text)
    for match in matches:
        year = int(match)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2025:
            return year
    return None

# Build mapping from numeric book ID to publication decade
book_decades = {}
valid_books = 0
for book in books:
    year = extract_year(book['details'])
    if year:
        decade = str(year // 10 * 10) + 's'
        # Extract numeric part from book_id (format: bookid_X)
        book_num_id = book['book_id'].split('_')[1]
        book_decades[book_num_id] = decade
        valid_books += 1

print('Books with valid publication years: {}'.format(valid_books))

# Join reviews with books using numeric ID mapping
joined_reviews = []
for review in reviews:
    # Extract numeric part from purchase_id (format: purchaseid_X)
    review_num_id = review['purchase_id'].split('_')[1]
    if review_num_id in book_decades:
        joined_reviews.append({
            'book_id': review_num_id,
            'decade': book_decades[review_num_id],
            'rating': float(review['rating'])
        })

print('Successfully joined {} reviews to books'.format(len(joined_reviews)))

# Group by decade and calculate statistics
decade_groups = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})
for review in joined_reviews:
    decade_groups[review['decade']]['ratings'].append(review['rating'])
    decade_groups[review['decade']]['books'].add(review['book_id'])

print('Found {} different decades'.format(len(decade_groups)))

# Calculate average ratings and filter by at least 10 distinct books
decade_results = []
for decade, data in decade_groups.items():
    num_books = len(data['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        decade_results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_ratings': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('Decades with at least 10 distinct books: {}'.format(len(decade_results)))
for i, result in enumerate(decade_results):
    avg_str = str(round(result['avg_rating'], 3))
    book_str = str(result['num_books'])
    rating_str = str(result['num_ratings'])
    print('{}. {}: avg={}, books={}, ratings={}'.format(
        i + 1, result['decade'], avg_str, book_str, rating_str
    ))

if decade_results:
    best_decade = decade_results[0]['decade']
    best_rating = decade_results[0]['avg_rating']
    print('\\nBest decade: {} with average rating {}'.format(best_decade, round(best_rating, 3)))
else:
    best_decade = None
    print('\nNo decades found with at least 10 books')

# Return the result in the required format
result_dict = {'best_decade': best_decade}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result_dict))

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load data from the stored files
books_file = var_functions.query_db_62
reviews_file = var_functions.query_db_64

with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print('Loaded {} books and {} reviews'.format(len(books), len(reviews)))

# Function to extract publication year from book details
def extract_year(details_text):
    # Find all 4-digit years in the text
    matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details_text)
    for match in matches:
        year = int(match)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2025:
            return year
    return None

# Build mapping from numeric book ID to publication decade
book_decades = {}
valid_books = 0
for book in books:
    year = extract_year(book['details'])
    if year:
        decade = str(year // 10 * 10) + 's'
        # Extract numeric part from book_id (format: bookid_X)
        book_num_id = book['book_id'].split('_')[1]
        book_decades[book_num_id] = decade
        valid_books += 1

print('Books with valid publication years: {}'.format(valid_books))

# Join reviews with books using numeric ID mapping
joined_reviews = []
for review in reviews:
    # Extract numeric part from purchase_id (format: purchaseid_X)
    review_num_id = review['purchase_id'].split('_')[1]
    if review_num_id in book_decades:
        joined_reviews.append({
            'book_id': review_num_id,
            'decade': book_decades[review_num_id],
            'rating': float(review['rating'])
        })

print('Successfully joined {} reviews to books'.format(len(joined_reviews)))

# Group by decade and calculate statistics
decade_groups = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})
for review in joined_reviews:
    decade_groups[review['decade']]['ratings'].append(review['rating'])
    decade_groups[review['decade']]['books'].add(review['book_id'])

print('Found {} different decades'.format(len(decade_groups)))

# Calculate average ratings and filter by at least 10 distinct books
decade_results = []
for decade, data in decade_groups.items():
    num_books = len(data['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        decade_results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_ratings': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('Decades with at least 10 distinct books: {}'.format(len(decade_results)))
for i, result in enumerate(decade_results):
    avg_str = str(round(result['avg_rating'], 3))
    book_str = str(result['num_books'])
    rating_str = str(result['num_ratings'])
    print('{}. {}: avg={}, books={}, ratings={}'.format(
        i + 1, result['decade'], avg_str, book_str, rating_str
    ))

if decade_results:
    best_decade = decade_results[0]['decade']
    best_rating = decade_results[0]['avg_rating']
    print('\\nBest decade: {} with average rating {}'.format(best_decade, round(best_rating, 3)))
else:
    best_decade = None
    print('\nNo decades found with at least 10 books')

# Return the result in the required format
result_dict = {'best_decade': best_decade}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result_dict))

    ratings_str = str(r['rating_count'])
    print(str(i+1) + '.', r['decade'], 'avg=' + avg_str, 'books=' + books_str, 'ratings=' + ratings_str)

best_decade = results[0]['decade'] if results else None
print('Best decade:', best_decade)

result_data = {'best_decade': best_decade}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result_data))

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re
import os
from collections import defaultdict

# Try to find the JSON files in /tmp and load them
print('Listing files in /tmp:')
files = os.listdir('/tmp')
print('Total files:', len(files))

# Find JSON files that look like our data files
json_files = [f for f in files if f.endswith('.json')]
print('JSON files found:', len(json_files))
for f in json_files[:5]:
    print('  ', f)

# Load the two most recent JSON files (they should be our data)
books = []
reviews = []

# Try to load the largest JSON files (likely our data)
if len(json_files) >= 2:
    # Sort by size
    json_files_sorted = sorted(json_files, key=lambda f: os.path.getsize(os.path.join('/tmp', f)), reverse=True)
    
    for i, filename in enumerate(json_files_sorted[:3]):
        filepath = os.path.join('/tmp', filename)
        filesize = os.path.getsize(filepath)
        print(f'\nFile {i+1}: {filename} (size: {filesize} bytes)')
        
        try:
            with open(filepath, 'r') as f:
                data = json.load(f)
                print(f'  Loaded {len(data)} records')
                if i == 0:
                    books = data
                elif i == 1:
                    reviews = data
                # Print first record structure
                if data and isinstance(data[0], dict):
                    print(f'  Keys: {list(data[0].keys())}')
        except Exception as e:
            print(f'  Error loading: {e}')

print(f'\nFinal counts - Books: {len(books)}, Reviews: {len(reviews)}')

if not books or not reviews:
    print('ERROR: Could not load data')
    result = None
else:
    # Extract decades from books
    book_decades = {}
    for book in books:
        details = book.get('details', '')
        years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
        for year_str in years:
            year = int(year_str)
            if 1900 <= year <= 2025:
                decade = str(year // 10 * 10) + 's'
                book_id_num = book['book_id'].split('_')[1]
                book_decades[book_id_num] = decade
                break

    print('Books with decades extracted:', len(book_decades))

    # Join with reviews
    joined = []
    for review in reviews:
        review_num_id = review['purchase_id'].split('_')[1]
        if review_num_id in book_decades:
            joined.append({
                'book_id': review_num_id,
                'decade': book_decades[review_num_id],
                'rating': float(review['rating'])
            })

    print('Reviews matched to books:', len(joined))

    # Group by decade
    groups = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})
    for item in joined:
        groups[item['decade']]['ratings'].append(item['rating'])
        groups[item['decade']]['books'].add(item['book_id'])

    print('Decades found:', len(groups))

    # Calculate statistics
    results = []
    for decade, data in groups.items():
        book_count = len(data['books'])
        if book_count >= 10:
            avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
            results.append({
                'decade': decade,
                'avg_rating': avg_rating,
                'book_count': book_count,
                'rating_count': len(data['ratings'])
            })

    results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)
    
    print('Decades with >=10 books:', len(results))
    for i, r in enumerate(results):
        print(f"{i+1}. {r['decade']}: avg={round(r['avg_rating'],3)}, books={r['book_count']}, ratings={r['rating_count']}")

    if results:
        best_decade = results[0]['decade']
        print('Best decade:', best_decade)
        result = best_decade
    else:
        print('No valid decades found')
        result = None

output = {'best_decade': result}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re
import os
from collections import defaultdict

# Find and load JSON files from /tmp
files = os.listdir('/tmp')
json_files = [f for f in files if f.endswith('.json')]

books = []
reviews = []

# Load the largest JSON files (likely our data)
if len(json_files) >= 2:
    json_files_sorted = sorted(json_files, key=lambda f: os.path.getsize(os.path.join('/tmp', f)), reverse=True)
    
    for i, filename in enumerate(json_files_sorted[:3]):
        filepath = os.path.join('/tmp', filename)
        
        try:
            with open(filepath, 'r') as f:
                data = json.load(f)
                if i == 0:
                    books = data
                elif i == 1:
                    reviews = data
        except Exception as e:
            continue

print('Books:', len(books), 'Reviews:', len(reviews))

# Extract decades from books
book_decades = {}
for book in books:
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', book['details'])
    for year_str in years:
        year = int(year_str)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2025:
            decade = str(year // 10 * 10) + 's'
            book_decades[book['book_id'].split('_')[1]] = decade
            break

print('Books with decades:', len(book_decades))

# Join reviews with books
joined = []
for review in reviews:
    review_num_id = review['purchase_id'].split('_')[1]
    if review_num_id in book_decades:
        joined.append({
            'book_id': review_num_id,
            'decade': book_decades[review_num_id],
            'rating': float(review['rating'])
        })

print('Reviews matched:', len(joined))

# Group by decade
groups = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})
for item in joined:
    groups[item['decade']]['ratings'].append(item['rating'])
    groups[item['decade']]['books'].add(item['book_id'])

# Calculate statistics
results = []
for decade, data in groups.items():
    book_count = len(data['books'])
    if book_count >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'book_count': book_count
        })

results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('Decades with >=10 books:', len(results))
for r in results:
    print(r['decade'], 'avg=' + str(round(r['avg_rating'], 3)), 'books=' + str(r['book_count']))

best_decade = results[0]['decade'] if results else None
print('Best decade:', best_decade)

result_output = {'best_decade': best_decade}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result_output))
